title: APRA DTI Limit Activation Notice — February 2026

sections.heading: Activation of DTI Limit — Effective Date and Scope
sections.content: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) hereby activates restrictions on new residential mortgage lending where the borrower's debt-to-income (DTI) ratio exceeds 6.0. This restriction takes effect from 1 February 2026 and applies to all Authorised Deposit-taking Institutions (ADIs) regulated under the Banking Act 1959. The DTI limit of 6.0 applies to all new residential mortgage originations from the activation date. An ADI must not extend new residential mortgage credit to any borrower whose DTI ratio would exceed 6.0 as a result of the new lending.

sections.heading: DTI Calculation Methodology
sections.content: Debt-to-income ratio is calculated by dividing the borrower's total committed debt obligations by their gross annual income. Total committed debt includes all outstanding loan balances, including home loans, investment property loans, personal loans, and credit card limits at 3.8% of the credit limit. Gross annual income includes salary, wages, and verified rental income. Income from a single contract employer is included only to the extent the contract term extends beyond 12 months from the assessment date. Where the income contract expires within 6 months of the assessment date, the ADI must calculate an effective post-contract DTI using the borrower's expected income after contract expiry. A DTI ratio of 7.2 constitutes a material breach of the February 2026 limit of 6.0.

sections.heading: Existing Portfolio — Identification and Documentation
sections.content: ADIs are required to identify all existing loans in their portfolio where the current borrower DTI exceeds 6.0. These pre-existing breaches must be documented within 5 business days of identification. Documentation must include: the borrower's identity, the current DTI ratio, the date the loan was originated, the income basis used at origination, and any changes in income since origination that have affected the DTI. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI of 7.2 must document this within 5 business days and report the identification to APRA through the standard prudential reporting framework. Failure to identify and document existing breaches within the required timeframe is a separate compliance obligation from the reporting of the breach itself.

sections.heading: Effective Future DTI — Income Expiry Assessment
sections.content: Where a borrower's primary income source is a single-employer contract with an expiry date within 6 months of the assessment date, the ADI must calculate the borrower's effective future DTI. The effective future DTI is calculated using the borrower's expected income after the contract expires, which may be nil, reduced, or uncertain depending on the borrower's employment situation. A borrower whose current DTI is 7.2 based on contract income, but whose income contract expires in 3 months, must have their effective post-contract DTI calculated and reported. If the effective future DTI exceeds 9.0 after income contract expiry, this represents a material deterioration requiring escalated monitoring and immediate remediation planning. The effective future DTI must be recorded in the ADI's BCA_DTI system records alongside the current DTI ratio.

sections.heading: Reporting Requirements — APRA Notification
sections.content: ADIs must report all identified DTI breaches to APRA within the standard Authorised Deposit-taking Institution Prudential Reporting Form ARF 223. Where a borrower DTI breach is identified after the 1 February 2026 activation date, the ADI must notify APRA within 5 business days of identification. The notification must include: the number of affected borrowers, the aggregate loan value, the distribution of DTI ratios, and the proposed remediation timeline. An ADI that identifies a borrower with a DTI ratio of 7.2 as of the activation date and has not yet reported this to APRA is in breach of its reporting obligations under the February 2026 DTI Limit Activation Notice. Unreported breaches attract additional regulatory scrutiny under the supervisory framework.

sections.heading: Remediation Requirements and Timeline
sections.content: Where an ADI identifies existing loans with DTI ratios above 6.0, the ADI must implement a monitoring plan within 15 business days of identification. The monitoring plan must include: quarterly DTI reassessment, income verification at each renewal or variation, and escalation triggers for DTI increases above 8.0. For borrowers with DTI ratios above 8.0 or approaching 9.0 due to income expiry, the ADI must implement active remediation including review of the loan structure and assessment of the need for additional security or guarantor support. APRA may require the ADI to reduce the DTI through additional repayments, loan restructuring, or the application of additional security. Remediation plans must be submitted to APRA within 30 business days of the identification of a material breach.
